If f A in (11)is square,$n\;X n,$ then (11) maps $R^{n}$ into $R^{n}$ If this Ａis nonsingular,so that $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ exists (see Sec. 7.8),then multiplication of ((11) by $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ from the left ta and use of $\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{I}$ $\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{I}$ gives the inverse transformation (14)$\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{y}.$ $\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{y}.$ $\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{y}.$ 
It maps every $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{y}_{0}$ onto that X,which by (11)is r mapped onto $\mathbf{y}_{0}$ The inverse of a linear transformation is itself linear,because it is given by a matrix, as (14) shows.

core_content.markdown:
## Linear Transformations (continued)

If f A in (11)is square,$n\;X n,$ then (11) maps $R^{n}$ into $R^{n}$ If this Ａis nonsingular,so that $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ exists (see Sec. 7.8),then multiplication of ((11) by $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ from the left ta and use of $\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{I}$ $\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{I}$ gives the inverse transformation (14)$\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{y}.$ $\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{y}.$ $\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{y}.$ 

It maps every $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{y}_{0}$ onto that X,which by (11)is r mapped onto $\mathbf{y}_{0}$ The inverse of a linear transformation is itself linear,because it is given by a matrix, as (14) shows.

rec_texts: because it is given by a matrix, as (14) shows.
Advanced Engineering Mathematics, 10/e by Edwin Kreyszig
Copyright 2011 by John Wiley & Sons. All rights reserved.
